caveats: a: No compensation-to-receipts ratio exists because this is a government code.
a: Public occupation data do not isolate government space-flight centers.
a: Current NASA AI uses must not be counted again as not-yet-automated opportunity.
rho: Bounded judgment rather than an observed implementation rate.
rho: Mission-support pilots do not establish safe use in flight-critical systems.
rho: Cost and schedule overruns can reflect hardware complexity beyond AI-relevant workflow friction.
e: Zero reflects an observed classification boundary, not an imputation for missing data.
e: A large commercial space economy exists, but its firms are classified in other industries.
s5: Agency leadership changes, reorganizations, program transfers, contractor recompetes, and privatization are not qualifying equity control transfers.
s5: The supplied LMM firm count is missing.
q: Public budget savings, mission acceleration, and scientific output are valuable but are not commercial operator retention as defined.
q: Benefits retained by government contractors belong to industries outside 927110.
d5: Government space GDP includes agencies and activities beyond NASA and 927110.
d5: Budget dollars mix price, hardware, grants, contracts, and civil-service production.
d5: Commercialization can preserve national space activity while reducing direct government operation.
o: Commercial operators may supply a growing share even when government remains the mission sponsor.
o: This primitive characterizes durable operator responsibility and does not override zero firm eligibility.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Government agencies and flight centers execute appropriated public missions and procure substantial contractor support rather than sell recurring services to arm's-length customers for commercial revenue; private aerospace R&D is classified outside 927110.
lens.excluded_activities: Private aerospace research and development, commercial space transportation, spacecraft and satellite manufacturing, government manufacturing establishments, defense activities classified elsewhere, nonprofit research institutions, financing vehicles, and entities outside the EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: The industry activity assessed for task and demand context is government administration and operation of spaceflight, space research, exploration, and space-flight centers; under the fixed LMM outsourced-service lens, no establishment qualifies.

a.bridge: BLS describes aerospace engineers across government and private employers rather than a wage-weighted 927110 task census, while NASA documents agency AI uses in mission support, research, image and archive analysis, autonomy, and planning. I map scientific literature and mission-data analysis, code and simulation assistance, proposal and requirements drafting, documentation, scheduling, risk triage, anomaly review, and program administration as exposed; novel hypothesis selection, accountable engineering judgment, physical integration and testing, launch and flight operations, independent verification, and safety certification remain less substitutable. The engineer proxy may omit scientists and administrative staff, while NASA examples include work already automated and therefore excluded from current opportunity.
caveats: No compensation-to-receipts ratio exists because this is a government code.
Public occupation data do not isolate government space-flight centers.
Current NASA AI uses must not be counted again as not-yet-automated opportunity.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted task inventories from NASA and other government flight centers, combined with code repositories, document workflows, compute logs, AI inventories, and measured time savings by occupation, would materially revise the estimate.
a.rationale: Space research is unusually digital and knowledge-intensive, but high-consequence hardware, validation, security, and mission authority bound the substitutable share.

d5.bridge: BEA reports that real GDP for the overall U.S. space economy grew 0.6 percent in 2023 and government space GDP grew 7.9 percent, while GAO reports a NASA portfolio of 38 major projects with at least 74 billion dollars of estimated life-cycle cost and increasing Artemis focus. I translate one-year economy-wide growth and a multi-year project pipeline into moderate central growth in five-year constant-quality government space research and flight-operation demand, with a wide range for appropriations, administration priorities, mission cancellations, commercial transition, cost overruns, schedule delays, geopolitics, and new Moon, Mars, nuclear-power, Earth-science, and discovery objectives. The BEA measure is broader than 927110 and annual growth can overstate persistence; project life-cycle cost is not five-year service quantity.
caveats: Government space GDP includes agencies and activities beyond NASA and 927110.
Budget dollars mix price, hardware, grants, contracts, and civil-service production.
Commercialization can preserve national space activity while reducing direct government operation.
d5.change_evidence: Enacted multi-year appropriations, the latest NASA spending plans by account, agency workforce and procurement plans, mission confirmation and cancellation decisions, GAO portfolio updates, and constant-dollar obligations for government space R&D would materially revise the range.
d5.rationale: National space objectives and a large mission pipeline support durable demand, but appropriations and program reprioritization can change the public research basket sharply within five years.

caveats: Zero reflects an observed classification boundary, not an imputation for missing data.
A large commercial space economy exists, but its firms are classified in other industries.
e.change_evidence: A future NAICS revision that includes private space R&D firms in 927110, or evidence that the dataset contains transferable private entities despite the official definition, would change this value.
e.rationale: The 2022 NAICS manual defines 927110 as government establishments and explicitly classifies private aerospace research and development in 541715. Government agencies and flight centers do not meet the fixed population of transferable LMM firms with normalized EBITDA.

l.detail: No receipts concept for this code (government / SUSB out of scope). QCEW government wages exist ($2,734,061,704) but there is no revenue denominator; labor_share left null.
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0000 (margins.json[92]: ESTIMATE: public administration — EBITDA is not meaningful for government units; margin 0 makes the n_band chain yield 0 by construction (recorded as a known gap)); revenue/employee $374,583 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at --); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: Commercial operators may supply a growing share even when government remains the mission sponsor.
This primitive characterizes durable operator responsibility and does not override zero firm eligibility.
o.change_evidence: Binding commercial-transition plans, civil-service function inventories, contractor insourcing or outsourcing data, delegated flight-authorization rules, and validated autonomous mission operations without government operational control would move the estimate.
o.rationale: Most year-five quantity still requires an accountable government operator to set public mission objectives, appropriate and steward funds, accept safety and scientific risk, oversee contractors, authorize flight, manage international and national-policy obligations, and preserve public data. AI can perform analysis and autonomous spacecraft functions, and commercial partners can operate more low-Earth-orbit services, but neither removes sovereign mission authority across the current basket.

caveats: Public budget savings, mission acceleration, and scientific output are valuable but are not commercial operator retention as defined.
Benefits retained by government contractors belong to industries outside 927110.
q.change_evidence: A verified population of private 927110 firms with recurring customer contracts, pricing, recompete, and benefit-sharing evidence would be required to estimate retention.
q.rationale: The code has no eligible commercial operator-customer revenue model under which implemented gross benefits could be retained after pricing, renewal, and competition.

caveats: Bounded judgment rather than an observed implementation rate.
Mission-support pilots do not establish safe use in flight-critical systems.
Cost and schedule overruns can reflect hardware complexity beyond AI-relevant workflow friction.
rho.change_evidence: Audited NASA and flight-center deployments measuring accepted code, analysis throughput, document time, scientific error, anomaly-detection precision, verification effort, security incidents, and sustained workflow adoption would move the range.
rho.rationale: Research assistance, archive analysis, coding, simulation support, drafting, knowledge retrieval, and program administration can be implemented within five years. Scientific reproducibility, export controls, classified or sensitive data, cybersecurity, procurement, legacy mission systems, explainability, verification and validation, long hardware cycles, and human accountability constrain realization.

caveats: Agency leadership changes, reorganizations, program transfers, contractor recompetes, and privatization are not qualifying equity control transfers.
The supplied LMM firm count is missing.
s5.change_evidence: Evidence of private transferable firms correctly classified in 927110, followed by denominator-based ownership and transaction records, would be required to estimate this primitive.
s5.rationale: There are no eligible firms under the fixed lens, so a probability of qualifying control transfer is undefined rather than zero.

principal_driver: Large digital archives and engineering workflows make government space research highly amenable to AI-assisted analysis and mission support.
principal_weakness: Official NAICS boundaries leave no transferable LMM firm or commercial value-capture mechanism inside 927110.
